rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF JESSE CHIHHSI CHEN
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Motion To Dismiss With Prejudice: Collusion And Fraud Claims In Chun Yen Chens Petition To Determine Title To Real Property Under Probate Code 850, Etc. (Added For Posting Of Tentative Rulings)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 2 of 7] Discussion The Motion seeks an order dismissing with prejudice the allegations of collusion and fraud contained in the Administrator's 850 Petition, on the basis of several theories: First, the Motion argues the allegations are "concealed" in the pleading, and an 850 Petition is exclusively for determining property rights. Second, the Motion argues the allegations are speculative and not supported by evidence. Third, the Motion argues the contract regarding the Popeyes franchise agreement is a forgery of their signatures, which undermines Administrator's credibility as to the Administrator's 850 Petition's other claims. Fourth, the Motion argues the Administrator's 850 Petition is retaliatory in nature, as it was filed after Mikki Chen's 850 petition. Fifth, the Motion argues the allegations of collusion and fraud complicate the process under Section 850 and cause the elderly Mikki Chen undue stress. Sixth, the Motion argues the Administrator's 850 Petition's omission of an Interspousal Transfer Deed, attached as Exhibit 3 to the Motion, indicates "the Petitioner's case lacks the completeness, relevance, and transparency required for a fair and just legal proceeding" (Motion at 6:15-16). Seventh, the Motion argues the allegation or suggestion in the Administrator's 850 Petition that the moving parties have malicious intent toward Decedent and his spouse are unfounded and implausible, given Mikki Chen's history toward the couple. Eighth, the Motion argues the allegations of collusion and fraud would put unnecessary strain on the court's resources. Ninth, the Motion argues the Administrator's 850 Petition's claims should be dismissed with prejudice because the Administrator's and her attorney's "derelictions of duties collectively cast doubt on their ability to ensure a fair, transparent, and just legal process" (Motion at 8:8-9). Tenth, the Motion argues Decedent and Administrator as his spouse had autonomy and control over "the course of events" and over Mikki Chen, "particularly within the company's operations," and thus the allegations of collusion and fraud are not credible (Motion at 8:14-25). The Motion's eleventh subsection states that Decedent and Mikki Chen disputed ownership of the business and rights to profits during Decedent's lifetime, and argues "This knowledge of longstanding disputes challenges the Petitioner's credibility of the collusion and fraud allegations and warrants the dismissal of such claims." (Motion at 9:12-15.) The Motion's twelfth subsection argues Administrator's actions and choice of attorney in her role as Administrator indicate that she is self-serving, and that supports the motion to dismiss the collusion and fraud claims. The thirteenth subsection of the Motion argues the terms of the August 6, 2007 Contract are clear and unambiguous, undercutting the Administrator's 850 Petition's allegations of collusion and fraud. The fourteenth subsection argues that the collusion and fraud claims fall within the August 6, 2007 Contract's exculpatory clause, and the clause should be upheld in dismissing the claims. The Motion's fifteenth subsection asks the court to dismiss the fraud and collusion claims to preserve family bonds and protect elderly family members, specifically Mikki Chen. The sixteenth subsection argues that Administrator has taken a confrontational approach and propounded burdensome discovery requests.

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF JESSE CHIHHSI CHEN
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Motion To Dismiss With Prejudice: Collusion And Fraud Claims In Chun Yen Chens Petition To Determine Title To Real Property Under Probate Code 850, Etc. (Added For Posting Of Tentative Rulings).
rulings.Rulings: [Part 5 of 7] When considering a demurrer, the trial court must accept as true all material facts properly pleaded in the complaint and those arising by reasonable implication. (Moore v. Conliffe (1994) 7 Cal. 4th 634, 638; Montclair Parkowners Ass'n v. City of Montclair (1999) 76 Cal. App. 4th 784.) A pleading or petition may claim rights the person is not actually entitled to or claim damages the person has not actually suffered - that is not the question before the court when considering a demurrer. A demurrer is not used to determine the merits of a claim. If the petition alleges facts addressing all the necessary elements of a claim or cause of action, it will survive a general demurrer. (See, Kauffman v. Bobo & Wood (1950) 99 Cal.App.2d 322; Shook v. Pearson (1950) 99 Cal. App. 2d 348, 351, "If any count or cause of action in a complaint setting up several counts or causes of action is good as against demurrer, a judgment of dismissal for insufficiency of the complaint cannot be sustained.") "[I]f, on consideration of all facts stated, it appears that the plaintiff is entitled to any relief at the hands of the court against the defendants, the complaint will be held good, although the facts may not be clearly stated, or may be intermingled with a statement of other facts irrelevant to the cause of action shown, or although plaintiff may demand relief to which he is not entitled under the facts alleged." (Augustine v. Trucco (1954) 124 Cal.App.2d 229, 236, quoting Matteson v. Wagoner (1905) 147 Cal. 739, 742.) Here, the Administrator's Petition is brought under Probate Code section 850. All title disputes and adverse claims to real and personal property involving the interests of a decedent's estate may be resolved in a probate proceeding under Probate Code section 850 et seq. Based on the face of the Administrator's 850 Petition, including the prayer, the Administrator has filed the petition seeking an order of the court determining the estate's interests in the disputed real and personal property under subsection (a)(2)(C) ("Where the decedent died in possession of, or holding title to, real or personal property, and the property or some interest therein is claimed to belong to another.") and subsection (a)(2)(D) ("Where the decedent died having a claim to real or personal property, title to or possession of which is held by another."). On the face of the petition, Administrator has pleaded sufficient facts to meet the essential elements of a claim under these Probate Code sections: that she is the personal representative of the estate, that the estate owns the disputed assets, and that Mikki Chen and/or Andy Chen and/or Does 1-50 either possess the assets or claim ownership of the assets. Whether these claims are true or meritorious is not the question on a demurrer. The Administrator's 850 petition pleads enough facts to state a cause of action and survive the general demurrer.

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF JESSE CHIHHSI CHEN
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Motion To Dismiss With Prejudice: Collusion And Fraud Claims In Chun Yen Chens Petition To Determine Title To Real Property Under Probate Code 850, Etc. (Added For Posting Of Tentative Rulings).
rulings.Rulings: [Part 3 of 7] The seventeenth subsection states the moving parties are experiencing mental and emotional distress caused by the Administrator's 850 Petition's allegations and prayer for monetary damages, and by the moving parties appearing self-represented against a represented party. The eighteenth subsection argues the prayer for actual damages of $1,000,000, plus double damages under section 859, is based on speculative damages because Administrator retains the disputed assets, and the claim for double damages is punitive and unwarranted. The nineteenth subsection states that Mikki Chen has made good faith efforts to resolve the dispute, by "propos[ing] relinquishing 60% of the estate's total appraised asset value in a previous petition" and, also, by not activating the August 2007 Contractual Agreement's reversion clause (Motion at 14:2-19). The final subsection of the Motion asserts Mikki Chen's right to pursue her interests in the disputed assets. The Motion presents several Exhibits in support of the Motion's legal assertions. Despite its length, the Motion does not cite statutory authority or case law in support of the request to dismiss the fraud and collusion claims with prejudice. As reflected in the Opposition to the Motion and in the Reply to the Opposition, the parties are also uncertain about the legal posture of the Motion and the legal authority under which the various requested forms of relief are being sought. Administrator opposes the Motion arguing that a motion to dismiss is the legal equivalent of a general demurrer, and the Motion fails to challenge the legal sufficiency of the Administrator's 850 Petition based on defects that appear either on the face of the pleading or from matters outside the pleading that are judicially noticeable. The Opposition objects to the Exhibits submitted with the Motion as impermissibly outside of the face of the pleading and judicial notice. In the Reply to the Opposition, captioned as a "rebuttal," the moving parties state they are seeking dismissal only of the fraud and collusion claims contained in the Administrator's 850 Petition, and not the entire petition. The Reply acknowledges that perhaps the Motion was incorrectly captioned as a motion to dismiss. The Reply then appears to argue the Motion both is and is not a general demurrer, but regardless, states the Motion is brought on the grounds of Administrator's failure to plead sufficient facts to constitute a cause of action for either collusion or fraud. The Reply argues the Exhibits demonstrate the claims' lack evidentiary support and may be considered under the doctrine of judicial notice. The moving parties again assert the defense of unclean hands against Administrator's 850 Petition on the basis of the 2016 Popeyes Agreements allegedly containing their forged signatures, and repeat the Motion's argument that the fraud and collusion claims should be dismissed because of the harm the litigation is causing the moving parties.

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF JESSE CHIHHSI CHEN
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Dismiss With Prejudice: Collusion And Fraud Claims In Chun Yen Chens Petition To Determine Title To Real Property Under Probate Code 850; While Preserving Mikki May Chiu Chens Rights Under Probate Code 850 For Determination Of Title To Real Property And Business Property Interests
rulings.Rulings: [Part 1 of 7] This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Stephen M. Murphy. Before the court is Mikki Chen and Andy Chen's Motion to Dismiss with Prejudice: Collusion and Fraud Claims in Chun Yen Chen's Petition to Determine Title to Real Property Under Probate Code section 850; While Preserving Mikki May Chiu Chen's Rights Under Probate Code section 850 For Determination of Title to Real Property and Business Property Interests. Procedural Background Chun Yen Chen ("Administrator"), Decedent's spouse, was appointed Administrator of Decedent's estate, with full authority under IAEA and with $2,205,000 bond, by this court on May 5, 2022. Letters issued on May 12, 2022. On August 2, 2022, Decedent's mother, Mikki May Chiu Chen, filed a creditor's claim for $2,300,000, which was rejected by Administrator. Mikki Chen subsequently filed an opposition to the May 10, 2023 Waiver of Accounting, Report of Administrator, Petition for Statutory Fees and Petition for Final Distribution. On October 27, 2023, Mikki May Chiu Chen filed an 850 petition alleging elder abuse and claiming 100% ownership of the following assets: A condominium at 855 La Playa, #462 San Francisco, CA 94121; 'Sweet Potato Enterprises, Inc.', owner of a Popeyes franchise located at 599 Divisadero Street, San Francisco, CA 94117; Equity in stocks: 2916 shares of Tesla and 310 shares of Apple. On November 1, 2023, Administrator filed an 850 Petition ("Administrator's 850 Petition") for the same assets, and seeking additional statutory damages pursuant to section 859, and civil and punitive damages: the title to the real property located at 855 La Playa, #462, San Francisco, California 94121; the stock ownership of Sweet Potato Enterprises, Inc.; and the ownership of 2916 shares of Tesla stock and 310 shares of Apple stock. The "Administrator's 850 Petition alleges Decedent's mother Mikki Chen, his brother Andy Chen, obtained a contract between the three family members for sale of the condo by Mother and Brother to Decedent, and sale of the business and the business' equipment by Mother and Brother to Decedent, and Mikki Chen in collusion with Andy Chen and Does 1-50 entered into this agreement in bad faith, "with intent to defraud and deceive" Decedent to perform under the contract during his lifetime, and to claim ownership of the assets after his death. (Pet. at paragraphs 21, 22.) On January 10, 2024, Mikki Chen and Andy Chen filed this motion, captioned Motion to Dismiss with Prejudice: Collusion and Fraud Claims in Chun Yen Chen's Petition to Determine Title to Real Property Under Probate Code section 850; While Preserving Mikki May Chiu Chen's Rights Under Probate Code section 850 For Determination of Title to Real Property and Business Property Interests. Additionally, on January 31, 2024, Mikki Chen and Andy Chen filed a Response to Administrator's 850 Petition, in part addressing the collusion and fraud allegations with an unclean hands defense. Chun Yen Chen filed an Opposition to the instant Motion on February 6, 2024, and a Response to Mikki Chen and Andy Chen's 850 Petition on February 7, 2024. Further hearing on the petition for final account and distribution, the competing 850 Petitions, and status of court-ordered mediation is currently set for March 20, 2024.

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF JESSE CHIHHSI CHEN
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Motion To Dismiss With Prejudice: Collusion And Fraud Claims In Chun Yen Chens Petition To Determine Title To Real Property Under Probate Code 850, Etc. (Added For Posting Of Tentative Rulings).
rulings.Rulings: [Part 6 of 7] Based on the face of the petition and the face of the prayer, Administrator is also seeking damages pursuant to Probate Code section 859 as well as civil damages. Administrator is seeking monetary damages of $1,000,000, ostensibly as a refund of the monies Decedent paid Mikki Chen under the 2007 Contract (Petition at paragraphs 20, 24), unspecified interest accrued, and unspecified punitive damages. Under Probate Code section 859, in relevant part, "If a court finds that a person has in bad faith wrongfully taken, concealed, or disposed of property belonging to [ ] the estate of a decedent, [ ] the person shall be liable for twice the value of the property recovered by an action under this part." This is in addition to "liability for reasonable attorney's fees and costs" in the court's discretion, as well as "any other remedies available in law to a person authorized to bring an action pursuant to this part." Again, whether the estate is entitled to these claimed damages is not the question on a demurrer. Instead, the question is whether the petition states enough facts to constitute a cause of action. The Administrator's 850 petition pleads enough facts to state a cause of action and survive the general demurrer. Whether the Petition's legal theory or claims are correct is not before the court in ruling on a demurrer. Assuming as true the facts properly pleaded and properly noticed in the Petition, facially brought pursuant to Probate Code section 850 et seq, the claims do not necessarily fail on the basis of failure to state a cause of action. Insofar as the Motion presents issues about discovery propounded on the moving parties, and alleges Administrator has breached fiduciary duties owed to the moving parties or the estate, those issues are not properly before the court for consideration on this Motion. The parties may seek relief on those issues under a separately appropriate noticed motion or petition. For the reasons discussed above, Mikki Chen and Andy Chen's Motion to Dismiss with Prejudice: Collusion and Fraud Claims in Chun Yen Chen's Petition to Determine Title to Real Property Under Probate Code section 850; While Preserving Mikki May Chiu Chen's Rights Under Probate Code section 850 For Determination of Title to Real Property and Business Property Interests is DENIED.

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF JESSE CHIHHSI CHEN
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Motion To Dismiss With Prejudice: Collusion And Fraud Claims In Chun Yen Chens Petition To Determine Title To Real Property Under Probate Code 850, Etc. (Added For Posting Of Tentative Rulings).
rulings.Rulings: [Part 4 of 7] "Judicial notice" is the court's recognition of the existence of a matter of law or fact, without the necessity of formal proof of the existence of a matter of law or fact. (See Evid. C. section 450 et seq.) Judicial notice is limited to the relevant facts properly subject to notice given the materials presented and does not, for example, extend to the truth of assertions in those materials. (See Richtek USA, Inc. v. UPI Semiconductor Corp. (2015) 242 Cal.App.4th 651, 659-660.) Neither party has properly requested the court take discretionary judicial notice of any matter pursuant to Evidence Code section 452. The moving parties have not given the Administrator sufficient notice of any request for judicial notice that the court could read into the Reply (see Evidence Code section 453(a)). The Exhibits submitted as part of the Motion are not matters subject to compulsory judicial notice under Evidence Code section 451. The court does not take judicial notice of any matters pursuant to either Evidence Code sections 451 or 452, and will not consider the Exhibits to the Motion. There are various statutes authorizing a motion to dismiss an action on specific grounds (e.g., Code of Civil Proc. sections 389(b), 583.210 et seq, 583.410 et seq). There are no statutes authorizing a motion to dismiss brought as a challenge to the merits of a pleading. A motion to dismiss based on extrinsic evidence must comply with the requirements for a motion for summary judgment (see, Saltarelli & Steponovich v. Douglas (1995) 40 Cal. App. 4th 1, 5). The Motion does not comply with the requirements for a motion for summary judgment or summary adjudication, and no party argues it was intended to be considered under that statute. As cited in the Opposition, some case law allows a "non-statutory" motion to dismiss to be treated as if it were a general demurrer to the pleading (see, Citizens for Parental Rts. v. San Mateo Cnty. Bd. of Educ. (1975) 51 Cal. App. 3d 1, 38; and Barragan v. Banco BCH (1986) 188 Cal. App. 3d 283, 299, both citing McKay v. County of Riverside (1959) 175 Cal.App.2d 247, 249, abrogated on unrelated grounds). Based on the statements and argument made in the moving parties' Reply to the Opposition (see Rebuttal at 3:4-18), and the cited case law, the court will review this Motion as a general demurrer to the Administrator's 850 Petition's collusion and fraud claims, on the grounds of Code of Civil Procedure section 430.10 ("pleading does not state facts sufficient to constitute a cause of action"). As a threshold issue, if the moving parties intended to bring this Motion as a demurrer, they failed to comply with the meet and confer requirements of Code of Civil Procedure section 430.41 prior to filing this Motion. While the court does not overrule the demurrer based on this defect, all parties are instructed to be apprised of the applicable law and to follow statutory procedures.

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF SHIU CHUNG LEE
rulings.Calendar Matter: ACCOUNTING PETITION RE: Report, Fees, Final Distribution
rulings.Rulings: Hearing. Appearance required. 1) The decedent, his mother, and his two siblings initially held title to the 9th Ave property and 45th Ave property as joint tenants. The petitioner alleges that, when the decedent's mother subsequently signed a deed transferring her 25% interest in each property to her trust, the joint tenancy was severed not only as to the decedent's mother's 25% interest, but also as to the remaining 75%, and was transformed into a tenancy in common among the decedent, his mother, and his two siblings, each holding an undivided 25% interest in each property as a tenant in common. But see Shelton v. Vance (1951) 106 Cal.App.2d 194, 196 ["The deed from Blanche to Virginia severed the joint tenancy as between the interest acquired by Virginia from Blanche and the interests held by Virginia and Theodore in joint tenancy. Virginia was then a tenant in common as to a one-third interest, and a two-thirds interest was held by her and Theodore in joint tenancy. Where one of three or more joint tenants conveys his interest to a third person, the latter then becomes a tenant in common, instead of a joint tenant, with the other, although such others remain joint tenants as between themselves."] If the decedent's mother's execution of the deeds transferring her 25% interests in the 9th Avenue property and 45th Avenue property to her trust severed the joint tenancy only as to her 25% interest in each property, did the decedent and his two siblings continued to hold the remaining 75% interest in each property as the original joint tenants, absent evidence of a subsequent severance in a manner recognized by Civil Code section 683.2? The deeds signed by the decedent's two siblings in 2022, about 10 years after the decedent's death, to transfer their interests in each property to their own trust, did not retroactively sever their joint tenancy with the decedent. 2) The petition proposes distribution of the decedent's interest in each real property to the beneficiaries in different shares. To the extent distribution is sought other than according to the laws of intestate succession, a written consent signed by all parties affected by the distribution must be filed. See LRSF 14.35(A). PARTIES APPEARING SHOULD CHECK IN FOR THE HEARING WITH THEIR FULL NAME. Counsel and parties may appear personally or remotely through Zoom or CourtCall. For a video appearance, go to zoom.us, click "join a meeting" and input meeting ID 160 225 4765 and password 514879. For an audio-only appearance through Zoom, call 1-669-254-5252 and key in meeting ID "160 225 4765#", then participant ID "0#", then password "514879#". (Toll rates may apply).) The Court prefers that counsel and parties appearing by video input their first and last name into the "Your Name" dialogue box. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling 1-888-882-6878. Please review LRSF 14.1(C) for more information.

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF CHIN HSIN LEE
rulings.Calendar Matter: PETITION FOR SPOUSAL PROPERTY
rulings.Rulings: Continued to 4/9/24. (No earlier availability.) Issue(s) necessitating continuance: 1. Now, another Amended Spousal Property Petition has been filed. It is unclear if this was intended to amend the SPP filed on 12/18/23 or to be a SECOND AMENDED SPP to replace the Amended SPP filed on 10/27/23. Neither the amended petition filed on 10/27/23 nor the new petition filed on 12/18/23 has been withdrawn or dismissed. 2. As noted previously, notice is defective if mailed before the filing of the pleading requiring notice. Since the Notice of Hearing was just of the "Amended Spousal Property Petition," and there are now two such pleadings, it is unclear which amended petition is meant. 3. The latest amended SPP was filed on 2/23/24, which was not filed at least 15 days before this hearing. If the decedent's parents' consents and waivers of notice were filed, the Court could waive procedural defects. Deadline for addressing these issues is 3/28/24. Examiner continuances are final and not subject to contest under LRSF 14.10 B.

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF CECIE WEST
rulings.Calendar Matter: PETITION FOR LETTERS OF ADMINISTRATION
rulings.Rulings: Continued to 4/3/24. (No earlier availability.) Issue(s) necessitating continuance: (1) No proposed order has been lodged or received by the assigned examiner. [LRSF 14.7 A. and 14.59 E.] (2) The correct Notice of Petition to Administer Estate showed the wrong hearing date (year). (3) Notice must include the procedures for making a remote appearance by Zoom and CourtCall, as well as remote public access, as set out at https://sf.courts.ca.gov/divisions/probate-court. [LRSF 14.1 C.6.] (4) The Acknowledgement of Receipt of the Duties and Liabilities must be filed. (5) The Confidential Statement of Birth Date and Driver's License Number (Judicial Council Form DE-147S) is also required, pursuant to Probate Code Section 8404(b). Form DE-147S must be filed separately and confidentially with a cover sheet. (6) When second generation or more distant heirs are listed, the deceased ancestor through whom they take (or would have taken) shall be named and his or her date of death and relationship to the decedent shall be stated. [LRSF 14.18 C.] What are the dates of death of the deceased children? (7) If a grandchild died, item 8 must clearly state whether or not she was survived by issue. Please provide a proposed order and courtesy copies of the petition and any papers filed to address these issues to the assigned examiner by 3/25/24. Examiner continuances are final and not subject to contest under LRSF 14.10 B. (NB: A petitioner seeking appointment must sign and verify her own pleadings, including any supplement or amendment to her petition. The register of actions indicates that a proof of publication is pending e-filing; it has not yet been reviewed.)
